flashcards.q: What are the three dimensions of food security?
flashcards.a: Availability, accessibility, and affordability.

flashcards.q: According to the 1995 World Food Summit, what does food security require?
flashcards.a: All people, at all times, must have physical and economic access to sufficient, safe and nutritious food to meet their dietary needs and food preferences for an active and healthy life.

flashcards.q: What concept did Amartya Sen add to the understanding of food security?
flashcards.a: The concept of 'entitlements'—a combination of what one can produce, exchange in the market, along with state or other socially provided supplies.

flashcards.q: What is a famine?
flashcards.a: A famine is characterised by widespread deaths due to starvation and epidemics caused by forced use of contaminated water or decaying food and loss of body resistance due to weakening from starvation.

flashcards.q: Which famine was the most devastating in India and when did it occur?
flashcards.a: The Bengal Famine of 1943, which killed thirty lakh people.

flashcards.q: Who were the worst affected groups during the Bengal Famine?
flashcards.a: Agricultural labourers, fishermen, transport workers, and other casual labourers.

flashcards.q: What is chronic hunger?
flashcards.a: A consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity and/or quality; poor people suffer from this due to very low income.

flashcards.q: What is seasonal hunger?
flashcards.a: Hunger related to cycles of food growing and harvesting; prevalent in rural areas due to seasonal agriculture and in urban areas due to casual labour.

flashcards.q: Name the three types of ration cards in India.
flashcards.a: Antyodaya cards (poorest of the poor), BPL cards (below poverty line), and APL cards (above poverty line).

flashcards.q: What is the full form of MSP?
flashcards.a: Minimum Support Price.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of declaring MSP before the sowing season?
flashcards.a: To provide incentives to farmers and assure them of a minimum guaranteed price for their crops.

flashcards.q: What is buffer stock?
flashcards.a: The stock of foodgrains (wheat and rice) procured by the government through FCI and stored in granaries.

flashcards.q: What is the Issue Price?
flashcards.a: The price lower than market price at which foodgrains from buffer stock are distributed to the poorer sections of society through PDS.

flashcards.q: What is the full form of FCI?
flashcards.a: Food Corporation of India.

flashcards.q: What is the Public Distribution System (PDS)?
flashcards.a: The system through which food procured by FCI is distributed through government-regulated ration shops among the poorer sections of society.

flashcards.q: What are ration shops also known as?
flashcards.a: Fair Price Shops.

flashcards.q: Approximately how many ration shops are there in India?
flashcards.a: About 5.5 lakh ration shops.

flashcards.q: What does RPDS stand for and when was it introduced?
flashcards.a: Revamped Public Distribution System, introduced in 1992 in 1,700 backward blocks.

flashcards.q: What does TPDS stand for and when was it introduced?
flashcards.a: Targeted Public Distribution System, introduced in 1997 to target the 'poor in all areas' with differential pricing.

flashcards.q: When was Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) launched and who does it target?
flashcards.a: Launched in December 2000, it targets the poorest of the poor BPL families.

flashcards.q: How much foodgrains does an AAY family receive per month and at what rates?
flashcards.a: 35 kg per month at `2 per kg for wheat and `3 per kg for rice.

flashcards.q: What is the Annapurna Scheme (APS)?
flashcards.a: A scheme launched in 2000 providing 10 kg of free foodgrains per month to indigent senior citizens.

flashcards.q: What is the National Food Security Act, 2013?
flashcards.a: An Act providing for food and nutritional security at affordable prices as a legal right; covers 75% of rural and 50% of urban population.

flashcards.q: Under NFSA, how much foodgrains does each eligible person receive per month?
flashcards.a: 5 kg per person per month.

flashcards.q: Under NFSA, what are the issue prices for wheat, rice, and coarse grains?
flashcards.a: `2 per kg for wheat, `3 per kg for rice, and `1 per kg for coarse grains.

flashcards.q: Name any three states that are major foodgrain surplus producers.
flashcards.a: Punjab, Haryana, and Uttar Pradesh.

flashcards.q: Which states have the largest number of food insecure people?
flashcards.a: Uttar Pradesh (eastern and south-eastern parts), Bihar, Jharkhand, Orissa, West Bengal, Chhattisgarh, parts of Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra.

flashcards.q: Name any two cooperative organisations that play a role in food security.
flashcards.a: Amul (Gujarat) and Mother Dairy (Delhi).

flashcards.q: What percentage of fair price shops in Tamil Nadu are run by cooperatives?
flashcards.a: Around 94%.

flashcards.q: What is the role of the Academy of Development Science (ADS) in Maharashtra?
flashcards.a: It facilitates a network of NGOs for setting up grain banks in different regions.

flashcards.q: What is a subsidy in the context of food security?
flashcards.a: A payment a government makes to a producer to supplement the market price of a commodity, keeping consumer prices low while maintaining higher income for producers.

flashcards.q: What are the two components of India's food security system?
flashcards.a: Buffer stock and Public Distribution System (PDS).

flashcards.q: What was the total foodgrain production in India in 2021-22?
flashcards.a: 315 million tonnes.

flashcards.q: Which two crops are primarily procured under the buffer stock system?
flashcards.a: Wheat and rice.

flashcards.q: What is the National De-addiction Helpline number mentioned in the chapter?
flashcards.a: This question does not pertain to the chapter content.
